summary: new RegExp rebuilt for every element scanned inside the findLastIndex predicate
suggestion: Hoist `const re = new RegExp(\`^\\s*${matchKind}\\s*>\`, "i");` out of the findLastIndex callback (matchKind is constant for this iteration), then reference `re` inside the predicate. The regex is currently reconstructed once per element of mergedTail on each newTail loop; hoisting builds it once per newTail.
rationale: matchKind is fixed within the enclosing for-loop iteration, so the RegExp value is identical for every mergedTail element. It is not a /g stateful regex, so hoisting one level does not change matching behavior. Pure allocation reduction, output-identical.

summary: Group-header regex captures a trailing group `(.*)` that is never read
suggestion: In both parseKmn (line 179) and parseFragment (line 243), the regex `/^\s*group\s*\(\s*(\S+?)\s*\)\s*(.*)/i` only ever uses capture group [1] (the name); groupMatch[2] is never referenced. The trailing `\s*(.*)` can be dropped to `/^\s*group\s*\(\s*(\S+?)\s*\)/i` without altering group [1] (the lazy `\S+?` already stops at the closing paren).
rationale: Grep confirms groupMatch[2] is unused in the file. Removing the trailing optional capture cannot change what capture group 1 matches, since `\s*\)` anchors the name capture. Behavior-preserving.

summary: parseKmn and parseFragment duplicate the group-block scanning loop (same header regex, same currentGroup accumulate/flush pattern)
suggestion: The core loop that recognizes a `group(<name>)` header, flushes the previous GroupBlock, and pushes subsequent lines into bodyLines is copy-pasted across both functions with the same regex and GroupBlock shape. A shared internal helper (e.g. a group-splitting generator over lines, with per-caller hooks for header/store handling) would remove the duplication. Left as needs-human because the two functions diverge (store extraction + bare-rule synthesis in parseFragment, header-region handling in parseKmn), so the extraction boundary needs judgment.
rationale: Genuine copy-paste-with-variation of the group-accumulation state machine. Extraction is behavior-preserving in principle but non-mechanical due to the divergent pre/post logic, so it should be reviewed rather than auto-applied.

summary: VFS-content decode idiom (typeof entry.content === "string" ? entry.content : new TextDecoder().decode(entry.content)) is duplicated across the codebase.
suggestion: Extract a shared helper (e.g. decodeVfsContent(entry) or entryContentAsString(vfs, path)) into a common module (pattern-apply or a shared VFS util) and call it here. The equivalent logic already exists privately as entryContentAsString() in packages/engine/src/compiler/index.ts:133-141 but is not exported; promoting/reusing it would consolidate all copies.
rationale: The same string-or-Uint8Array decode branch appears in applyKeycapLabelsToVfs.ts (lines 100-102, 211-213, 277-279), compiler/index.ts (139-140, private), output/zip.ts (78), and loader code. No exported shared helper exists. Behaviour-preserving: identical decode semantics. Marked needs-human because the fix requires creating/exporting a cross-file helper, not a purely local edit, and must not alter vfs.get/set semantics.

summary: Local escapeRegExp is a byte-identical copy of scaffolder's escapeForRegex and emit.ts's inline metachar escaper.
suggestion: Extract one shared regex-metachar escaper (e.g. a small util exported from packages/engine/src) and import it here, in scaffolder/index.ts, and codec/emit.ts instead of maintaining three copies. Behavior is identical (same /[.*+?^${}()|[\]\\]/g body).
rationale: escapeRegExp (lines 394-396) body `s.replace(/[.*+?^${}()|[\]\\]/g, "\\$&")` is identical to escapeForRegex in packages/engine/src/scaffolder/index.ts:84 and the inline `storeName.replace(/[.*+?^${}()|[\]\\]/g, "\\$&")` in packages/engine/src/codec/emit.ts:394. None are exported, so this is a cross-file extraction (needs-human) rather than a drop-in import of an existing symbol. The vendored escapeStringForRegex in simulator/vendor/.../util.ts has different (Unicode-escape) semantics and is NOT a substitute. No shared xmlEscape exists (only the inverse unescapeXml in codec/parse-kvks.ts), so the local xmlEscape is not a reuse candidate.

summary: Index out-of-range validation is copy-pasted verbatim across the nul-fill and drop branches.
suggestion: Extract a small helper (e.g. `isIndexInRange(idx, store, warnings)` or a filter that pushes the identical out-of-range warning) and call it from both branches. The warning message text is byte-identical in both places (lines 375-378 and 403-406), so factoring it out preserves output exactly.
rationale: Both branches iterate indexSet, apply the same `idx < 0 || idx >= store.items.length` guard, and push the same `[store-slot] index ${idx} out of range ...` warning. Consolidating removes duplication with no behavioral change (same warnings, same order per branch, same skips).

summary: In the drop branch, `storeApplied` is redundant state — it is always equal to `dropIndices.size`.
suggestion: Drop the `storeApplied` counter in the drop branch and use `dropIndices.size` for the `=== 0` guard (line 421) and the `appliedCount += ...` (line 449).
rationale: `storeApplied++` (line 418) happens on exactly the same iterations as `dropIndices.add(idx)` (line 417), and nothing else mutates either, so `storeApplied === dropIndices.size` at every use site. Substituting `dropIndices.size` is behavior-preserving. (The nul-fill branch has no such set, so its own `storeApplied` must stay.)

summary: `baseIr.stores.find(...)` runs a fresh linear scan of every store on each targeted-store iteration (O(targets x stores)).
suggestion: Build one `Map<string, IRStore>` keyed by `nodeId` from `baseIr.stores` before the loop and look up `storeNodeId` in it, collapsing the repeated scans into a single build pass.
rationale: collectCharContributors.ts (line 78) already establishes the by-store-map pattern (`new Map(ir.stores.map(s => [s.name, s]))`); here it would be keyed by nodeId. Lookup results are identical (first-match / present-or-undefined) since nodeIds are unique, so output and the 'store not found' warning path are unchanged. Pure hot-path allocation win, no semantic change.

summary: The output-element loop conflates two independent checks (does the rule output contain an index(), and is this store an emit target) under one nested `if`.
suggestion: Flatten to two independent guards: `if (el.kind === "index") ruleOutputHasIndex = true;` and `if ((el.kind === "index" || el.kind === "outs") && el.storeRef === storeName) flags.asEmitOutput = true;`. Removes the `ruleOutputHasIndex || el.kind === "index"` self-referential OR on line 161.
rationale: Behavior-preserving: `ruleOutputHasIndex` still becomes true iff any output element is an index(), and `asEmitOutput` still becomes true iff an index()/outs() element references this store. Purely a readability restructuring; no change to computed flags.

summary: The new-sub-key literal { nodeId: minter.mint("touchKey"), id: charToUnicodeKeyId(char), text: char } is written verbatim three times (longpress sk, flick, multitap).
suggestion: Extract a local closure, e.g. `const mintSubKey = (char: string): TouchKeyIR => ({ nodeId: minter.mint("touchKey"), id: charToUnicodeKeyId(char), text: char });`, and call it at each of the three sites. Because the closure mints only when invoked and is invoked at the exact same sequential point each block reaches today, NodeIdMinter mint order is unchanged. Do NOT fold in the touch_key_replace block (lines 248-253): it deliberately does not mint a nodeId (reuses key.nodeId via ...rest) and drops `output`, so it is not the same construction.
rationale: Verified against the raw-JSON sibling (applyTouchAssignmentsToRawJson.ts lines 228/235/244) which builds { id, text } without nodeId, so no cross-file helper exists to reuse; this is a same-file local extraction. Mint order preserved since the mint call stays at the identical call site.

summary: The getWorkingKey-not-found guard and its identical warning string are copy-pasted in all four handler blocks (longpress, flick, multitap, touch_key_replace).
suggestion: Hoist a helper such as `function resolveHostKey(hostKey: string, char: string): TouchKeyIR | undefined { const k = getWorkingKey(hostKey); if (!k) warnings.push(`[touch-apply] host key "${hostKey}" not found in phone default layer — assignment for "${char}" skipped`); return k; }` and replace each block's guard with `const key = resolveHostKey(hostKey, char); if (!key) continue;`. The warning text and control flow are byte-identical across all four sites, so behavior (warning order/content and skip semantics) is preserved.
rationale: All four warning strings are identical; the `continue` stays at the call site so control flow is unchanged. needs-human only because it touches four branches at once.

summary: The longpress_alternates and multitap handler blocks are near-identical — both read hostKey/char, resolve the key, dedupe existing entries via isTouchSubKeyDuplicate, mint a sub-key, and append; they differ only in the field name (sk vs multitap).
suggestion: Consolidate the two blocks into one parameterized path keyed on the target field (e.g. select field name 'sk' | 'multitap', read `key[field] ?? []`, dedupe, then `{ ...key, [field]: [...existing, newSubKey] }`). Both branches mint exactly one touchKey per applied char at the same point, so combining them keeps mint order identical for any given assignment stream (each assignment still fires exactly one branch). Note longpress carries the extra hint comment (lines 154-156) which is documentation-only and can be dropped/relocated without behavior change.
rationale: The two blocks match structurally except the sk/multitap field and dedupe target; both use the same isTouchSubKeyDuplicate predicate. Flagged needs-human/medium because the reader may prefer keeping the explicit per-mechanism blocks for clarity.

summary: Reconstruction re-derives the target slot by index comparison (idx === defaultLayerIndex, idx === phonePlatformIndex), but the resolved references defaultLayer/phonePlatform are already in scope.
suggestion: The .map reconstructions can compare by reference (`layer === defaultLayer ? updatedDefaultLayer : layer` and `platform === phonePlatform ? updatedPhonePlatform : platform`), which removes reliance on the two *Index locals for reconstruction. Behavior is identical because each id ("default", "phone") was located via findIndex on the same arrays, so the reference at that index is unique. Marginal; only worth doing if the index locals become otherwise unused.
rationale: Behavior-preserving but low value — the findIndex locals are still needed for the earlier not-found guards, so this does not eliminate them. Included for completeness per recall benchmark.

summary: matchedPlatforms collects platform names then re-looks-up keyMap+key; collapse to one pass carrying the key.
suggestion: Build `const matched: Array<{ pName: string; key: RawKey }> = []` in the first loop (push `{ pName, key: keyMap.get(hostKey)! }` when `keyMap.has(hostKey)`), gate the warning on `matched.length === 0`, then iterate `matched` directly using `key` — eliminating the redundant `platformDefaultKeyMaps.get(pName)!` and `keyMap.get(hostKey)!` re-lookups (lines 175-176).
rationale: Map iteration order is stable across both scans, so matched order equals the current matchedPlatforms order; the non-null assertions are already justified by the has() check. Output, warnings, and per-platform apply order are unchanged.

summary: defaultHint loop re-validates `platform && typeof platform === 'object'`, but every member of platformsGainingSk is already a validated RawPlatform.
suggestion: platformsGainingSk only ever receives platform names via applyLongpress, and those names come exclusively from platformDefaultKeyMaps keys, which were only inserted after the object/Array.isArray(layer)/default-layer guards at lines 123-136. The re-check at lines 201-202 can never be false, so the guard (and the local `platform`/`p` re-derivation) is redundant defensive code; the body can operate directly on `layout[pName] as RawPlatform`.
rationale: Behavior-preserving: the guard always passes for these keys, so removing it cannot change output. Marked needs-human because it relies on the invariant that platformsGainingSk ⊆ validated platforms — a reviewer should confirm no other code path adds to that set.

summary: Explicit `comments: baseIr.comments` is redundant with the `...baseIr` spread
suggestion: The object literal already opens with `...baseIr`, which copies `comments` (and `header`) verbatim. The trailing `comments: baseIr.comments` re-assigns the identical reference, so it can be dropped without changing output or reference identity. Kept only as documentation of intent; removal is behavior-preserving but sacrifices that explicit callout.
rationale: `...baseIr` at line 45 spreads every key of baseIr, including `comments`. Re-stating `comments: baseIr.comments` assigns the same value/reference, producing an identical result object. header (also passed through) is left implicit via the spread, so comments is inconsistently explicit.

summary: item.value.normalize('NFC') is recomputed for every store item on every rule that references that store.
suggestion: Precompute a per-store map of NFC-normalized item values (or the set of matching slot indices for `target`) once, keyed by store.nodeId, before the group/rule walk, then look it up inside the loop. `target` is already normalized once at line 70, so only the store side re-normalizes redundantly.
rationale: The base-layer alphabet fan-out and deadkey fan-out stores are commonly referenced by many rules; each reference re-runs `.normalize('NFC')` over the whole store.items array. Caching the normalization (or the matching indices) per store collapses O(rules x items) normalizations to O(items). Output is identical because target is already NFC and the comparison is unchanged. Adds a cache structure, so human review of placement is warranted.

summary: Output-side extraction uses split('>').slice(1).join('>'), which allocates an array and rejoins just to drop text before the first '>'.
suggestion: Replace with `const gt = frag.sourceText.indexOf('>'); const outputSide = gt === -1 ? '' : frag.sourceText.slice(gt + 1);`
rationale: Behavior is identical: with no '>', split(...).slice(1) is [] and join is '' (matches gt===-1 -> ''); with one or more '>', join('>') reconstructs everything after the first '>' exactly as slice(indexOf+1) does. Avoids the intermediate array/join allocation and reads clearer.

summary: `rule.output as { kind: string; value?: string; storeRef?: string }[]` widens the already-typed OutputElement[] discriminated union with a hand-written structural cast.
suggestion: Iterate `rule.output` directly as OutputElement[] and narrow via `el.kind` (the union already carries value on {kind:'char'} and storeRef on {kind:'index'|'outs'}). The `el.value ?? ''` at line 167 and `el.storeRef !== undefined` at line 146 become discriminant-guarded property reads.
rationale: OutputElement (packages/contracts/src/keyboard-ir.ts:50) is a discriminated union that already provides these fields per-kind, so the loose cast is over-defensive. Behavior is unchanged, but removing the cast requires restructuring the reads to satisfy the checker (e.g. filtering by kind before property access), which is why it needs human review rather than an automatic rewrite.

summary: `const isTriggerRule = isDeadkeyOnlyOutput;` is a rename-only alias for the imported predicate.
suggestion: Optional: call isDeadkeyOnlyOutput directly at line 135 and drop the alias, or keep the alias purely for the local S-02 doc comment. No behavior change either way.
rationale: The alias adds an indirection whose only value is the attached doc comment explaining the S-02 trigger-rule semantics. Inlining is behavior-preserving; kept low-confidence/needs-human because the doc comment is arguably worth preserving in place.

summary: Orphan block comment (lines 1-4) duplicates the real JSDoc on parseSlotId (lines 9-20).
suggestion: Delete the floating /** ... */ comment at lines 1-4. It describes parseSlotId but is not attached to any declaration (a blank line and the SLOT_ID_RE const separate it from the function), and its text is a strict subset of the canonical JSDoc at lines 9-20. Removing it is comment-only and behavior-preserving.
rationale: The comment is documentation-only; deleting it cannot alter output, timing, return type, or error type. The single authoritative JSDoc for parseSlotId already lives at lines 9-20, so the leading block is redundant.

summary: The `match[1] === undefined || match[2] === undefined` guard is runtime-dead — both capture groups are non-optional so they are always defined when exec() returns non-null.
suggestion: SLOT_ID_RE = /^(.+)#(\d+)$/ has two mandatory (non-optional, non-alternated) capture groups, so a non-null match always has defined match[1] and match[2]. The undefined checks never fire at runtime. They exist only for TypeScript narrowing (match[n] is typed string | undefined). Leaving as-is is safe; if simplified, the checks must be replaced with non-null assertions (match[1]!, match[2]!) to preserve types — hence needs-human. Do not remove the `match === null` check, which is a genuine guard.
rationale: Runtime behavior is identical whether the undefined branches are present or replaced by assertions, since the groups cannot be undefined for this regex. Flagged needs-human because the branch does real compile-time type-narrowing work and the net simplification is marginal. CAVEAT: under strict/noUncheckedIndexedAccess the capture groups are typed string|undefined, so the guard is load-bearing for the typed return — removing it requires a non-null assertion, which is why this is low-confidence/needs-human rather than a clean deletion.
